text: For most sports, there is no evidence that intense training and specialization before puberty are necessary to achieve elite status.
Early sports specialization is associated with higher rates of injury in young athletes.
Early sports specialization is associated with increased psychological stress and burnout in young athletes.
Early sports specialization is associated with higher rates of quitting sports at a young age.
Intense single-sport training to the exclusion of other sports should be delayed until late adolescence to optimize success while minimizing injury, psychological stress, and burnout.
Some degree of sports specialization is necessary to develop elite-level skill, but it occurs along a continuum rather than as an all-or-nothing choice.
